chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction

cards.back: A type conversion from a smaller numeric type to a larger numeric type that is value-preserving (no data loss) and safe. Includes integral promotion and floating-point promotion.

cards.text: A numeric promotion is {{c1::value-preserving}}, meaning no data or precision is lost in the conversion.

cards.front: What types are covered by integral promotion?
cards.back:
Integral promotion converts smaller integral types to int or unsigned int:
  • signed char, short → int
  • unsigned char, unsigned short → int (if int can hold all values) or unsigned int
  • char → int or unsigned int (implementation-defined)
  • bool → int (false=0, true=1)

cards.text: Integral promotion converts smaller integral types to {{c1::int}} or {{c2::unsigned int}}.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: bool values are promoted to int when used with std::cout. true promotes to 1, and !true (false) promotes to 0.

cards.back: The conversion of float to double. This is the only floating-point promotion in C++.

cards.text: The only floating-point promotion in C++ converts {{c1::float}} to {{c2::double}}.

cards.question: What type conversion happens in this code?
cards.answer: Both short operands a and b are promoted to int via integral promotion. The addition is performed on int values, and the result is type int.
cards.explanation: The binary arithmetic operator + requires both operands to be the same type. Since short is smaller than int, both operands are promoted to int before the addition.

cards.front: Why do numeric promotions exist in C++?
cards.back: Processors are typically optimized for operations on a specific size of data (often int or double). Promotions allow smaller types to be converted to the processor's preferred size for more efficient operations.

cards.explanation:
Unsigned types promote based on whether int can hold all values:
  • If int can hold all values: promote to int
  • If int cannot hold all values: promote to unsigned int
This depends on the size of the type and the size of int on the system.
examples: unsigned char → int  // int can hold 0-255
unsigned short → int // int can hold 0-65535

cards.text: When bool is promoted to int, {{c1::false}} becomes {{c2::0}} and {{c3::true}} becomes {{c4::1}}.

cards.front: Are numeric promotions safe to use?
cards.back: Yes. Numeric promotions are always safe because they are value-preserving - no data or precision is lost during the conversion.

cards.text: When binary operators work with operands smaller than int, the operands are first {{c1::promoted to int}} via integral promotion.
Numeric promotions are {{c1::value-preserving}} and {{c2::safe}}, meaning they never lose data or precision.
